primary_diagnosis.name: Thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger's disease) with critical limb ischaemia and digital gangrene

comorbidities.name: Heavy current tobacco use (approximately 30 cigarettes/day for 15 years)

pathophysiology_summary: Thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger's disease) is a non-atherosclerotic, segmental, inflammatory thrombotic occlusive disease affecting small and medium-sized arteries and veins of the distal extremities, almost exclusively in young to middle-aged heavy tobacco/nicotine users. Unlike atherosclerotic peripheral arterial disease, proximal vessels (femoral, popliteal) remain patent, while distal vessels (digital, tibial, pedal) become progressively occluded by inflammatory thrombus, producing a classic pattern of preserved proximal pulses with absent distal pulses. The disease process is intimately and causally linked to ongoing tobacco exposure — continued smoking drives relentless progression, while cessation is the only intervention proven to halt it. In this patient, 6 months of exertional claudication (arch pain with walking) reflects early distal arterial insufficiency; the evolution over 48 hours to unremitting rest pain and frank dry gangrene of the second toe represents critical limb ischaemia — a limb-threatening emergency. A history of intermittent tender, red, cord-like lesions on the lower legs is consistent with recurrent superficial (migratory) thrombophlebitis, a well-recognised associated feature of Buerger's disease. Absent tissue perfusion beyond a critical threshold leads to irreversible cell death (gangrene); without urgent revascularisation assessment and, critically, permanent smoking cessation, the risk of progressive tissue loss and major limb amputation is very high.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe rest pain and digital colour change (blackening) in the context of a background of exertional foot claudication over 6 months raises immediate concern for critical limb ischaemia of some cause; must be actively investigated even before smoking history is elicited.

Claudication progressing to critical limb ischaemia with digital gangrene is a recognised end-stage presentation of atherosclerotic PAD and remains on the differential until age and pulse pattern are established.
Diabetic microvascular and neuropathic disease can present with foot ischaemia and tissue loss; needs active screening for diabetes history.
Acute limb ischaemia from embolism can present with sudden severe pain and digital ischaemia; needs to be considered particularly given the acute-on-chronic worsening over 48 hours.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor must not dismiss this as 'just bad claudication' or minor foot pain. A black toe plus unremitting rest pain is a limb-threatening emergency regardless of eventual specific diagnosis and must trigger urgent escalation from the first turn.

differential.diagnosis: Thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger's disease) with critical limb ischaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Young patient (34) with heavy, long-standing tobacco use (DI-001) + progression from exertional claudication to rest pain and digital gangrene over 48 hours (DI-002) + visible black, cold second toe (DI-003) + history of migratory superficial thrombophlebitis-type lesions (DI-004) + no diabetes (DI-005) together form a classic Buerger's disease constellation distinguishing it from atherosclerotic and diabetic causes.

Age 34 is atypically young for atherosclerotic PAD to cause critical limb ischaemia in the absence of major risk factors like diabetes or hyperlipidaemia; the pattern of preserved proximal with absent distal disease (clinician exam finding, sealed) further argues against typical atherosclerosis, though this cannot be fully excluded via telehealth history alone.
No personal or family history of diabetes (DI-005) and pain is severe rather than painless (as diabetic neuropathic ulcers typically are), making diabetic aetiology unlikely.

Still cannot be fully excluded via history alone given the acute worsening; requires urgent vascular assessment including pulse examination and imaging to distinguish from a thromboangiitis obliterans flare.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After eliciting smoking history, symptom progression, digital colour change, and superficial phlebitis history, the AI Doctor should identify a probable non-atherosclerotic small-vessel occlusive process strongly linked to smoking, and recognise this as a limb-threatening emergency regardless of the exact final label.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger's disease) with critical limb ischaemia and digital gangrene
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context. Cannot be confirmed without clinician vascular examination (pulse mapping), and imaging (e.g. angiography for corkscrew collaterals). Emergency escalation for vascular surgery assessment is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final differential in the telehealth context should be framed as probable critical limb ischaemia, most consistent with Buerger's disease given the smoking history and age, requiring emergency vascular assessment — not downgraded to 'just claudication' or managed with reassurance and analgesia alone.

red_flags.description: Heavy current tobacco use — approximately 30 cigarettes/day for 15 years
red_flags.clinical_significance: Tobacco/nicotine exposure is both the primary causative agent and the key disease-modifying factor in thromboangiitis obliterans. Continued smoking drives ongoing vascular inflammation and thrombosis; this is not simply a risk factor to note but the central mechanism requiring urgent behavioural intervention.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Smoking history not identified as central to the pathological process; cessation not emphasised as urgent; ongoing tobacco exposure continues to drive vascular occlusion, worsening tissue loss.

red_flags.description: Progression from exertional claudication over 6 months to unremitting rest pain over the last 48 hours
red_flags.clinical_significance: Progression from claudication (adequate perfusion at rest, inadequate with exertion) to rest pain indicates critical limb ischaemia — a marker of severely compromised perfusion insufficient even for resting metabolic demand, historically associated with high risk of tissue loss without urgent intervention.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Rest pain not recognised as a marker of critical limb ischaemia; case managed as routine claudication with conservative advice; ongoing ischaemia leads to progressive gangrene and eventual major amputation.

red_flags.description: Second toe has turned completely black and cold over the preceding day
red_flags.clinical_significance: Visible tissue necrosis (gangrene) is objective evidence of irreversible tissue death and confirms the limb is in a critical, time-sensitive state; any delay in escalation risks progression of gangrene, superimposed infection, and loss of viable tissue proximal to the current line of demarcation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Gangrene not recognised as an emergency; patient advised to monitor at home; gangrene progresses and/or becomes infected, requiring more extensive amputation or risking systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Absent dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses on the right, severely diminished on the left, with palpable proximal (femoral/popliteal) pulses bilaterally, and capillary refill >5 seconds in the right foot
red_flags.clinical_significance: This clinician-performed vascular examination finding (distal pulse mapping and capillary refill) is the defining pattern distinguishing a distal small-vessel occlusive process like Buerger's disease from proximal atherosclerotic disease, and objectively confirms critical limb ischaemia. It cannot be obtained by the patient via telehealth chat and sits entirely on the sealed side.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth encounter — this finding can only be obtained on clinician examination; the correct telehealth action is to ensure the patient reaches that examination urgently.

red_flags.description: Corkscrew-appearing collateral vessels on peripheral angiography
red_flags.clinical_significance: This clinician-obtained imaging finding is a classic radiological sign of thromboangiitis obliterans (corkscrew collaterals reflecting small-vessel occlusion with collateral formation) and supports the diagnosis; it cannot be obtained or reported by the patient over telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth encounter — imaging can only be obtained in a clinical/hospital setting as part of the urgent vascular work-up.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Peripheral vascular disease is stereotypically associated with older patients. AI Doctor may under-weight the severity of a young patient's foot pain, assuming vascular causes are unlikely at age 34, and fail to recognise thromboangiitis obliterans as a specific young-smoker vascular emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on analgesia as the primary request
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Patient's stated priority is pain relief. AI Doctor may focus the response on analgesia advice without recognising and escalating the underlying limb-threatening ischaemia and tissue necrosis.

AI Doctor may treat smoking cessation as routine lifestyle advice to be addressed 'later' rather than recognising it as an urgent, disease-modifying intervention that is directly and causally linked to ongoing tissue loss in this specific condition.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failure to recognise rest pain and gangrene as critical limb ischaemia
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may interpret worsening foot pain as simply 'bad claudication' rather than recognising the transition to rest pain plus visible gangrene as objective critical limb ischaemia requiring emergency (not routine or semi-urgent) referral.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Peripheral pulse examination (femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial) — essential to distinguish proximal (atherosclerotic) from distal (thromboangiitis obliterans) disease patterns
Capillary refill time and formal assessment of tissue viability/demarcation line
Peripheral angiography — required to visualise corkscrew collaterals and confirm the vascular pattern
Formal vascular surgical assessment of amputation level/limb salvage potential
Blood pressure and heart rate measured by a clinician
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent emergency department presentation (self-transport is acceptable if the patient is otherwise stable and can travel promptly, but ambulance should be arranged if pain is disabling or transport is not readily available) for vascular surgery assessment, analgesia, and urgent imaging — do not delay until routine hours
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating this as routine claudication or providing only pain relief advice without urgent vascular referral risks progression of dry gangrene to wet gangrene/infection, systemic sepsis, and loss of a greater amount of limb tissue than would otherwise be necessary, or major limb amputation.
